title: 🎙️ Working Lands & Industry
description: Today's focus is the resource economy that defines the Cariboo — forestry, ranching, mining, and the agricultural technology helping these industries adapt and modernize. We're looking for stories about the people and innovations keeping rural BC's working landscape viable, including tensions between resource extraction and long-term stewardship, and the technology bridging both. - Daily themed article selection for podcast generation

_podcast.theme_description: Today's focus is the resource economy that defines the Cariboo — forestry, ranching, mining, and the agricultural technology helping these industries adapt and modernize. We're looking for stories about the people and innovations keeping rural BC's working landscape viable, including tensions between resource extraction and long-term stewardship, and the technology bridging both.
_podcast.theme_scoring_prompt: Rate how well this article fits the theme 'Working Lands & Industry' through a RURAL TECH AND INNOVATION lens. Strong fits include: precision forestry and remote sensing in Cariboo logging, AgTech and ranch management systems, mining automation and safety technology, farm sensors and soil monitoring, sawmill modernization and automation, drone surveys for resource management, IoT in agriculture and ranching, climate adaptation tech for working lands, workforce training for industrial tech, commodity tracking and supply chain tech, and how Cariboo resource industries adopt innovation. Prefer articles that bridge resource economy AND technology in rural/industrial contexts. Score 0-100 where 100 is a perfect thematic match and 0 is completely unrelated.

items.title: [Boing Boing] Man who stole $11 million from prison has a new trick: escaping
items.content_html:
Arthur Cofield was already famous for running an $11 million fraud scheme from prison with contraband phones, gold coins, and a private security company. Now he has improved the story by not being in prison.

The United States Marshals Service, the Federal Bureau of Investigation, and other law enforcement agencies are searching for 34-year-old Arthur Cofield.

— Read the rest

The post Man who stole $11 million from prison has a new trick: escaping appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [New Atlas] SR-71 successor? Quarterhorse Mk 2.1 prototype cracks the sound barrier
items.content_html:
The venerable Cold War SR-71 Blackbird may be looking nervously at its laurels after Hermeus's latest Quarterhorse Mk 2.1 uncrewed prototype broke the sound barrier at Spaceport America over the White Sands Missile Range airspace in New Mexico in March 2026.

items.content_html: Macie Stankoven (Image Credit: Contributed) By Marty Hastings STANLEY CUP PLAYOFFS Proud sister Stankoven in Raleigh for Stanley Cup Final, loving life among Caniacs Jun 1, 2026 | 6:30 PM Share on Facebook Share on Bluesky Share on X Copy Link KAMLOOPS — Macie Stankoven became accustomed in childhood to the cacophony emanating from the garage while her big brother Logan blasted tunes and shots – countless, endless shots. “Yeah, definitely,” Macie said with a laugh. “The music was so loud and the bar was always hitting. He used to always show me some tips when I used to play hockey, as well.”

items.title: [Boing Boing] Loony Toon sentenced to 20 years in prison for shooting at cops
items.content_html:
A man was sentenced to 20 years imprisonment after admitting he took shots at police in Milwaukie, Oregon, while fleeing a traffic stop. Loony Tune, 43, pleaded guilty to four counts of first-degree attempted assault and other crimes. Already a convited felon, the sentence was a plea deal arranged with Clackamas County District Attorney's Office. — Read the rest

The post Loony Toon sentenced to 20 years in prison for shooting at cops appeared first on Boing Boing.

items.title: [Boing Boing] Jack Daniel's sold a preacher story and buried the enslaved distiller
items.content_html:
For 150 years, Jack Daniel's sold the tidy story of a white country preacher teaching a boy to make whiskey. The man actually running the still was Nearest Green, an enslaved master distiller whose work gave Tennessee whiskey its signature trick. — Read the rest
